domains.evidence_snippet: This domain serves as a disguise, presenting itself as a ChatGpt site to lure victims into downloading a fake offline version of ChatGpt.

file_hashes.evidence_snippet: The main payload has the SHA256 hash fb7883d3fd9347debf98122442c2a33e.
The dropper payload has the MD5 hash cb533957f70b4a7ebb4e8b896b7b656c.
The final payload has the SHA256 hash 642dbe8b752b0dc735e9422d903e0e97.

urls.evidence_snippet: This URL initiates the download of a file called setupbrowser.exe, which is part of the malicious payload.

tools.name: RedEnergy Stealer
tools.evidence_snippet: The malware analyzed has dual capabilities as both a stealer and ransomware, representing an alarming evolution in ransomware attacks.

ttps.evidence_snippet: The malware masquerades as a browser update, depicted in Fig. 7 below.
The malware hijacks browser sessions, allowing it to steal sensitive information from the victim's browser.
The malware uses obfuscation techniques to evade detection and hinder analysis.
The malware disables or modifies tools to evade detection and hinder analysis.

summary: Zscaler ThreatLabz has discovered a new malware variant, RedEnergy stealer, that fits into the hybrid Stealer-as-a-Ransomware threat category. The malware uses a fake update campaign to target multiple industry verticals and possesses the ability to steal information from various browsers, enabling the exfiltration of sensitive data.

timeline.evidence_snippet: Zscaler ThreatLabz has discovered a new malware variant, RedEnergy stealer, that fits into the hybrid Stealer-as-a-Ransomware threat category.
